details.text: Derivative: قُلْ (Qul) “Say” when used in the Qur’an indicates that it is a lesson or message for the Messenger ﷺ and also a lesson or message that he must convey to others.

Derivative: أَحَدٌ (Ahad) – The difference between أَحَدٌ (Ahad) and وَاحِد (Waahid – One). أَحَدٌ is used in a manner that implies negation of others and exclusivity, like in the statement: “لَا أَحَد” (Laa Ahad) which means “No one” and is also used when you employ the statement “one of something” like “أَحَدُكُمْ” (one of you). وَاحِد (Waahid – One) can be said to be used in a positive and affirming manner. It is used like the English “one” to attach a number to something, like the statement “there is one person in the shop” or “one plus one equals two”. The use of أَحَدٌ (Ahad) with a positive meaning is unique to Allah and has not been used in Arabic literature in this manner. أَحَدٌ Ahad has been defined as the One who is unique (or alone and singular in uniqueness), who has no rival or peer and has no one alongside Him i.e., the One concerning whom no second is possible. It is a unique concept of the oneness of Allah that removes any possible comparison being made with Him. If Allah is separated from all attributes of human beings and creation you reach the conception of أَحَدٌ (Ahad). It is an attribute that cannot be shared by anyone or anything else or used to describe them. The use of أَحَدٌ (Ahad) in the نَكِرَة (Nakrah – indefinite) form can imply in Arabic emphasis like an exclamation mark! It also fits the form of the Khabar in the nominal sentence in the case where it provides the information regarding the subject or answers the question about who the subject is i.e., “Who is Allah? He is Ahad!”.

Derivative: هُوَ “He” – A pronoun is usually used when someone is talking about something that is already known and been identified. However, because of the universally known greatness of the subject the pronoun can be used. The use of هُوَ (Huwa - He) indicates to the greatness of Allah.

قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

- When you have two statements which are Khabar (predicate) then they are both linked to the Mubtada’ (Subject) i.e., both items of information are linked to the subject to produce the meaning of: “Say: He is Allah, He is Ahad (One)” which can be translated as “Say: He is Allah, Ahad (One)”.

Derivative: الصَّمَدُ – The definition of الصَّمَدُ is the sufficient, the One they return to whenever they have a need of Him. The One who is sufficient for them and meets their needs and answers all their questions they attribute to Him at the time of need. Linguistically صَمَدَ as a verb means to turn towards someone or to ask them for the fulfilment of a need. المَصْمُود (Al-Masmood) which is the implied meaning of الصَّمَدُ (As-Samad) is the one to whom the people turn towards at the time of need. This is the first meaning. Another meaning for الصَّمَدُ is to make someone your goal in terms of aspiring to reach them or attain them. When you attribute something as your goal, they become الصَّمَدُ (As-Samad). The two meanings mean that Allah is the One we turn to in the time of need and also that He is the ultimate goal. الصَّمَدُ (As-Samad) can also mean the One who is not in need of anyone else, the one who none can be higher than in status or in any attribute and who has no flaw. It has been used by the Arabs in respect to a person that cannot be overcome. It has also been stated by scholars of Tafsir that it refers to being great or magnificent in glory and majesty (عَظِيمُ الجَلَالة – ‘Azheem ul-Jalaalah), that it means the One who is always (دائِم – Daa’im) and forever (خَالِد - Khaalid) i.e. everlasting. Another linguistic aspect is that it can refer to something that is solid with no emptiness or holes inside i.e., it is perfectly saturated and filled. It implies complete and absolute without any flaw. A summary of the majority of explanations is that الصَّمَدُ (As-Samad) means: “The One whom everyone needs but does not need anyone himself”. The use of أَلِف لام (الْ) is for the purpose of اِسْتِغراق (Istighraq) which implies absoluteness.
Derivative: كُفُوًا means comparable like a rival who can go toe to toe with his counterpart. It was used for someone who was married from the same social standing i.e., an equal who is comparable.

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌ

- The order of the sentence structure has been altered from the normal Arabic structure which would be: وَلمْ يَكُنْ أَحَدٌ كُفُوًا لَهُ “And he did not have (وَلمْ يَكُنْ) anyone (أَحَدٌ) comparable to Him (لَهُ)”. Instead of being placed at the end لَهُ “to him” has been placed at the beginning and this style is used in Arabic to place that which is most important first i.ee., here the attribution to Allah. This advancement and early placement of لَهُ “to him” also implies that this is special to Allah i.e., that Allah is alone in not having anyone who can possibly be comparable to him. Everything else in existence can have a كُفُوًا (something comparable or its like and equal).
